network.overall_strategy: The synthesis network for arginine is primarily based on the cyanosulfidic protometabolism pathway in surface environments, as detailed in Patel et al. (2015). This 10-step sequence starts from simple precursors like acetylene, HCN, NH3, cyanamide, and H2S, proceeding through nitrile additions, guanidine formation, pyrimidine cyclization (with variants), ring-opening, thioamide chemistry, amination, and nitrile hydrolysis to yield arginine. To incorporate environmental interconnection, H2S is formed in hydrothermal environments via serpentinization and sulfide mineral interactions, feeding into surface reactions; HCN can be transported from hydrothermal sources to surface pools. Products from surface synthesis feed into biochemical environments for further assembly into peptides, though the core network focuses on arginine production. The network features convergent pathways at key hubs via cyclization variants, ensuring redundancy in prebiotic scenarios.

reactions.name: Acetylene + HCN to Acrylonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide to acetylene, forming the C2-C1 bond. The resulting vinyl nitrile (acrylonitrile) retains both unsaturation and the nitrile electrophilic center for subsequent Michael addition.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction initiates the carbon skeleton building as described in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile + Ammonia to 3-Aminopropanenitrile
reactions.mechanism: Michael addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile. NH3 adds to the β-carbon of the conjugated system in a 1,4-fashion, yielding the 3-amino nitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This step introduces the amine functionality, following Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: 3-Aminopropanenitrile + Cyanamide to 2-(2-Cyanoethyl)guanidine
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of the primary amine to the nitrile carbon of cyanamide. The amine nitrogen attacks the electrophilic C≡N of cyanamide, forming the guanidine (amidine) linkage that becomes arginine's characteristic side-chain functional group.
reactions.reasoning: This establishes the guanidine motif central to arginine, as per Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Dry Cyclization: 2-(2-Cyanoethyl)guanidine to 4-Hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine
reactions.mechanism: Intramolecular cyclization — the terminal guanidine nitrogen attacks the nitrile carbon, forming the C-N bond that closes the six-membered pyrimidine ring. Water from the medium (or intramolecular) hydrolyzes the resulting imine at C4 to give the hemiaminal (hydroxyl at C4).
reactions.reasoning: Variant A (dry cyclization) facilitated by Cu and H2S, as described in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic Cyclization: 2-(2-Cyanoethyl)guanidine + H2O to 4-Hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine
reactions.mechanism: Same cyclization as dry variant with explicit water as co-substrate for nitrile hydrolysis during ring closure.
reactions.reasoning: Variant B (hydrolytic cyclization) using Cu and H2S, following Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Hydrolytic Cyclization with NH3 Release: 2-(2-Cyanoethyl)guanidine + H2O to 4-Hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine + NH3
reactions.mechanism: Same cyclization as hydrolytic variant with ammonia released as a byproduct of the nitrile-to-hemiaminal conversion.
reactions.reasoning: Variant C (hydrolytic with NH3 release) mediated by Cu and H2S, as in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: 4-Hydroxy-2-imino-hexahydropyrimidine + HCN to Amino((3-cyano-3-hydroxypropyl)amino)methaniminium
reactions.mechanism: Ring-opening by HCN — cyanide attacks the hemiaminal carbon (C4) of the pyrimidine ring, breaking the C-N bond and opening the ring. The product is a linear α-cyanohydrin bearing the guanidine side chain. This ring-opening/cyanation effectively extends the carbon chain by one unit while introducing a new nitrile handle for subsequent transformations.
reactions.reasoning: This step extends the chain via ring-opening, per Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Nitrile Thiolysis: Amino((3-cyano-3-hydroxypropyl)amino)methaniminium + H2S to 4-(Diaminomethylideneamino)-2-hydroxybutanethioamide
reactions.mechanism: H2S adds across the nitrile triple bond (C≡N → C(=S)NH2). This is the hallmark cyanosulfidic reaction — sulfide acts as a nucleophile on the nitrile carbon, converting it to a thioamide. The hydroxyl group at the adjacent carbon is preserved.
reactions.reasoning: Thioamide formation via H2S addition, as detailed in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Reductive Dehydroxylation: 4-(Diaminomethylideneamino)-2-hydroxybutanethioamide to 4-(Diaminomethylideneamino)butanethioamide + H2O
reactions.mechanism: Cu/H2S-mediated reductive elimination of the hydroxyl group as water. The thioamide group facilitates this by stabilizing the developing carbocation or radical intermediate at the adjacent carbon. The net result is deoxygenation of the carbon skeleton.
reactions.reasoning: Dehydroxylation enabled by Cu/H2S, following Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Thioamide-to-Nitrile Conversion: 4-(Diaminomethylideneamino)butanethioamide + H2O + HCN to 2-(4-Cyano-4-hydroxybutyl)guanidine + H2S + NH3
reactions.mechanism: Complex exchange reaction — the thioamide is hydrolyzed/displaced, releasing H2S and NH3, while HCN provides a new nitrile group at the terminal carbon. Water participates in the hydrolysis of the thioamide and hydroxylation of the carbon. This reaction effectively cycles between thioamide and nitrile functional groups, a key feature of cyanosulfidic chemistry that enables carbon chain rearrangement.
reactions.reasoning: Functional group cycling via Cu/H2S, as in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Amination: 2-(4-Cyano-4-hydroxybutyl)guanidine + NH3 to 2-(4-Amino-4-cyanobutyl)guanidine
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic substitution — ammonia displaces the hydroxyl group at C4, converting the α-hydroxynitrile (cyanohydrin) to the α-aminonitrile. This introduces the α-amino group that will be present in the final amino acid product. The reaction is thermodynamically driven by the greater stability of the C-N bond relative to the C-O bond in the aminonitrile context.
reactions.reasoning: Introduction of the α-amino group, per Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202).

reactions.name: Nitrile Hydrolysis: 2-(4-Amino-4-cyanobutyl)guanidine + 2 H2O to L-Arginine + NH3
reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydrolysis — water attacks the nitrile carbon of the α-aminonitrile, forming an amide intermediate (α-aminoamide), which undergoes further hydrolysis to the carboxylic acid (α-amino acid) with release of ammonia. This is the same Strecker-type nitrile hydrolysis that produces other amino acids (alanine, serine, glycine) from their respective aminonitrile precursors under aqueous cyanosulfidic conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Final conversion to the amino acid, as described in Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). This product feeds into biochemical assembly.

reactions.name: Speculative Hydrothermal Production of H2S from Mackinawite
reactions.mechanism: Hydrolysis and reduction of iron sulfide minerals under hydrothermal conditions releases H2S, providing a prebiotic source for surface cyanosulfidic chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Speculative reaction necessary to connect hydrothermal H2S production to surface pathways; supported by general iron-sulfur world chemistry (e.g., Russell et al., 2014), but not specific to arginine synthesis in provided literature.

pathways.name: Dry Cyclization Pathway to Arginine
pathways.description: Main cyanosulfidic pathway using dry cyclization variant for pyrimidine formation.

pathways.name: Hydrolytic Cyclization Pathway to Arginine
pathways.description: Variant using explicit water in cyclization step.

pathways.name: Hydrolytic Cyclization with NH3 Release Pathway to Arginine
pathways.description: Variant releasing NH3 during cyclization, recycling ammonia.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Connected Pathway via H2S to Arginine
pathways.description: Incorporates hydrothermal H2S production feeding into surface cyanosulfidic pathway (using P1).

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Connected Hydrolytic Pathway to Arginine
pathways.description: Hydrothermal H2S into hydrolytic variant P2.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-Connected NH3 Release Pathway to Arginine
Alternative Upstream via Recycled NH3 in Dry Pathway
pathways.description: Uses recycled NH3 from potential side reactions, but follows P1 core.

pathways.name: Thioamide-Focused Pathway with H2S Recycle
pathways.description: Emphasizes H2S recycling in later steps, using P2.

pathways.name: Guanidine Hub Pathway via P3
pathways.description: Converges at guanidine intermediate with NH3 release.

pathways.name: Full Interconnected Pathway to Biochemical Assembly
pathways.description: Surface arginine production feeding into biochemical peptide formation (speculative extension).

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Dry cyclization variant aligns perfectly with literature; strong coherence and feasibility in surface conditions.
suggestions: Explicitly note Cu/H2S agents for cyclization.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrolytic cyclization variant matches literature variants; excellent alignment.
NH3 release variant directly from literature; high fidelity.
Incorporates hydrothermal H2S production; enhances interconnection.
Hydrothermal connection with hydrolytic variant; solid progression.
Full environmental flow with NH3 release; well-integrated.
Recycled NH3 adds redundancy; coherent but slightly speculative.
H2S recycle emphasizes thioamide focus; aligns with cyanosulfidic cycling.
Guanidine hub via P3; excellent use of convergence.
Full interconnection to biochemical; comprehensive environmental flow.

evaluation.network_feedback: Excellent network aligning perfectly with Patel et al. (2015); robust topology, feasibility, and literature support. Minor enhancements in agent specification and environmental details.
